buggy_code:
import pandas as pd

# Raw unadjusted prices — contains splits
prices = pd.read_csv('raw_prices.csv', parse_dates=['date'], index_col='date')

# Compute returns directly from unadjusted prices
returns = prices['close'].pct_change()

# This will show -50% return on split date for a 2-for-1 split
# and +100% return on reverse split
print(returns.describe())
clean_code:
import pandas as pd

# Use split-adjusted prices for return calculation
prices = pd.read_csv('adjusted_prices.csv', parse_dates=['date'], index_col='date')

# Returns from adjusted prices correctly handle splits
returns = prices['adj_close'].pct_change()

# No artificial return spikes from corporate actions
print(returns.describe())
description: Returns computed from unadjusted prices containing unhandled stock splits.
explanation: A 2-for-1 stock split halves the price overnight. Without adjustment, pct_change() shows a -50% return on the split date followed by +100% on the reverse (when prices recover). These are not real economic returns. Any momentum, mean-reversion, or volatility model trained on unadjusted returns will learn from these phantom returns.
suggested_fix: Always use split-adjusted (and dividend-adjusted) closing prices for return calculations in backtesting.

issues.title: Returns computed from unadjusted prices contain split artifacts
